excellent: Recognizes the corpus as NTSB general-aviation accident reports and enumerates a broad set of distinct occurrence types drawn from the narratives, including at minimum: in-flight loss of control / aerodynamic stall (and stall/spin), loss of control on the ground / ground loop / runway excursion, collision with terrain (CFIT) and/or water, collision with objects/obstacles (trees, brush, wires/power lines, structures), loss of engine power / powerplant or system malfunction, forced landing, hard landing (incl. bounced/porpoise/PIO), landing-gear collapse or gear-up landing, nose-over/roll-over, and midair collision (plus credit for fire, birdstrike/animal strike, turbulence upset, component/structural failure)

Clearly states that fatal/serious injuries are most strongly associated with in-flight loss of control / stall-spin, CFIT or collision with terrain/water, midair collisions, and low-altitude wire/obstacle strikes (high-energy in-flight occurrences)

Clearly states that damage-only / no-injury outcomes are typical of hard landings, gear collapse / gear-up landings, ground loops / runway excursions, and nose-overs / taxi or ground-handling events (low-energy ground- and landing-phase occurrences)

Supports the fatal-vs-damage distinction with at least one or two concrete examples from the corpus (e.g., AS 350 with 4 fatal, PA-32R midair 3 fatal, Cirrus SR22 pattern LOC 3 fatal; versus gear collapse / ground loop / hard landing cases with 'Highest injury: None')
Notes the link between occurrence type and energy state / flight phase (in-flight, high-speed, uncontrolled attitude vs. near-ground, low-speed)
good: Lists most of the major occurrence types (loss of control/stall, collision with terrain, engine power loss/forced landing, hard landing, gear collapse/gear-up, ground loop/runway excursion, nose-over, midair) even if a few minor categories are missing

Correctly identifies that in-flight loss of control/stall-spin and collision with terrain/water (and midairs) are the fatal/serious ones, while landing/ground-phase events (hard landing, gear collapse, ground loop, nose-over) are damage-only
May give fewer or only one specific example, and approximate framing of the energy/flight-phase rationale is acceptable
grading_criteria.partial: Names only a handful of occurrence types or gives a generic 'pilot error / loss of control' summary, and makes only a vague or partially correct distinction between fatal and damage-only occurrences without concrete examples.
grading_criteria.weak: Fails to enumerate meaningful occurrence types, misidentifies which occurrences cause fatalities (e.g., claims hard landings or gear collapses are the main fatal events), or fabricates categories/figures not grounded in the corpus.

key_facts: Corpus is NTSB aviation accident/incident reports, mostly Part 091 general aviation (also Part 135 and Part 137), covering airplanes, helicopters, and gliders

In-flight loss of control / aerodynamic stall and stall/spin is a dominant occurrence type (failure to maintain airspeed on takeoff, approach, landing, go-around, or maneuvering; Vmc/Vmca roll in twins)
Collision with terrain (CFIT) and collision with water (e.g., dark-night VFR over water) are represented occurrence types
Loss of control on the ground / ground loop / runway excursion ('veered off the runway') and nose-over are common damage-only occurrence types
Hard landing (bounced/porpoise/pilot-induced oscillation, practice autorotation) and landing-gear collapse / gear-up (wheels-up) landing are common no-injury occurrence types
Loss of engine power (fuel exhaustion, fuel starvation, carburetor/induction icing, mechanical failure) leading to forced landings is a key occurrence type
Other occurrence types include midair collision, wire/power-line and pole strikes, birdstrike/animal collision (crow, deer), turbulence upset, structural/component failure, and post-impact fire
Fatal/serious examples include the Eurocopter AS 350 B2 (4 fatal, 1 serious), the PIPER PA-32R midair collision (3 fatal), and the Cirrus SR22 traffic-pattern loss of control (3 fatal)
Fatal/serious injuries concentrate in high-energy in-flight occurrences (stall/spin, LOC-I, CFIT/water impact, midair, wire/obstacle strikes), often with aircraft 'Destroyed' and post-impact fire

Damage-only (Highest injury: None) outcomes concentrate in low-energy, near-ground occurrences (hard landings, gear collapse/gear-up, ground loops/runway excursions, nose-overs, taxi/ground events), typically with 'Substantial' damage
